agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Critical SQL injection vulnerability: raw user input from HTTP query params is interpolated directly into SQL via f-string."
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "SQL Injection via f-string query construction",
      "risk": "An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL through customer_id or status_filter, enabling full database read/write/delete, authentication bypass, and potential remote code execution depending on DB engine.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries function, lines 2-6 (query construction)",
      "reproduction": "Call the public API with: customer_id='; DROP TABLE deliveries; -- or status_filter=' OR '1'='1  — the first destroys data, the second dumps all rows for all customers.",
      "fix": "Use parameterized queries: query = 'SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = ? AND status = ?'; result = db_conn.execute(query, (customer_id, status_filter))",
      "time_to_fix": "5 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Security, data integrity, authorization — any customer can read or destroy any other customer's delivery data."
    },
    {
      "title": "No input validation on public-facing parameters",
      "risk": "Even with parameterized queries, passing unconstrained strings for status_filter allows queries against unexpected values. customer_id has no format check (e.g., UUID validation), making enumeration trivial.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries function signature / call site",
      "reproduction": "Pass status_filter=anything or iterate customer_id=1,2,3,... — no validation rejects bad input before it hits the DB.",
      "fix": "Validate customer_id format (e.g., UUID regex) and constrain status_filter to an allow-list of known statuses (e.g., 'pending', 'delivered', 'cancelled'). Return 400 for invalid values.",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization and API correctness — prevents parameter abuse and reduces attack surface."
    },
    {
      "title": "No authorization check — any caller can query any customer's deliveries",
      "risk": "The function trusts the caller-supplied customer_id with no verification that the authenticated user owns that customer_id. This is an IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) allowing cross-customer data access.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries function — missing authz gate before query execution",
      "reproduction": "Authenticate as customer A, then call the endpoint with customer_id=<customer B's ID>. All of customer B's deliveries are returned.",
      "fix": "Extract the authenticated user's customer_id from the session/token and use that instead of (or to validate against) the query parameter. Never trust caller-supplied identity for data scoping.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization — violates principle of least privilege; exposes all customer data to any authenticated user."
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "SELECT * returns all columns including potentially sensitive internal fields",
      "description": "Using SELECT * may leak internal columns (e.g., internal_notes, cost_price, driver_ssn) to the public API response. Explicitly enumerate only the columns the API consumer needs.",
      "location": "search_deliveries query"
    },
    {
      "title": "No pagination or result limit",
      "description": "A query matching thousands of rows will return them all in memory at once. For a public API, this enables denial-of-service by requesting a broad status like 'delivered'. Add LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based pagination.",
      "location": "search_deliveries query and return"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Return a typed data class or dict instead of raw DB row tuples to decouple the API response from the schema",
    "Add logging/metrics on query execution time to detect abuse patterns",
    "Consider adding an index on (customer_id, status) if one doesn't exist — this will be a hot query path"
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "This code is actively exploitable the moment it faces public traffic. SQL injection is the #1 web vulnerability (OWASP A03:2021 Injection).",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe but insufficient — if this code has already been deployed, the database may already be compromised. An incident review and audit are needed.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Switching to parameterized queries is backward-compatible for all legitimate callers. Only malicious payloads will stop working.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No error handling or logging exists. A failed injection attempt or malformed query will surface as an unhandled DB exception with no audit trail.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["auth_security: SQL injection enables full DB compromise; IDOR enables cross-customer data access"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the endpoint or take it offline immediately. If previously deployed, audit DB logs for injection patterns and rotate credentials."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 99,
    "high_confidence_areas": ["SQL injection is unambiguous and trivially exploitable", "IDOR is confirmed by the stated call pattern (customer_id from query params)", "No parameterization or validation exists anywhere in the function"],
    "low_confidence_areas": ["Cannot verify if authorization exists at a middleware layer above this function — but the function itself has none"],
    "assumptions": ["No ORM or query-sanitization layer wraps db_conn.execute", "customer_id and status_filter reach this function unsanitized as stated in the prompt"]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Straightforward code review of a small function with obvious SQL injection vulnerability.

Classification: `none` — the fix is a straightforward code change (parameterized queries).

content.text:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Critical SQL injection vulnerability: raw user input from HTTP query params is interpolated directly into SQL via f-string."
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "SQL Injection via f-string query construction",
      "risk": "An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL through customer_id or status_filter, enabling full database read/write/delete, authentication bypass, and potential remote code execution depending on DB engine.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries function, lines 2-6 (query construction)",
      "reproduction": "Call the public API with: customer_id='; DROP TABLE deliveries; -- or status_filter=' OR '1'='1  — the first destroys data, the second dumps all rows for all customers.",
      "fix": "Use parameterized queries: query = 'SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = ? AND status = ?'; result = db_conn.execute(query, (customer_id, status_filter))",
      "time_to_fix": "5 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Security, data integrity, authorization — any customer can read or destroy any other customer's delivery data."
    },
    {
      "title": "No input validation on public-facing parameters",
      "risk": "Even with parameterized queries, passing unconstrained strings for status_filter allows queries against unexpected values. customer_id has no format check (e.g., UUID validation), making enumeration trivial.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries function signature / call site",
      "reproduction": "Pass status_filter=anything or iterate customer_id=1,2,3,... — no validation rejects bad input before it hits the DB.",
      "fix": "Validate customer_id format (e.g., UUID regex) and constrain status_filter to an allow-list of known statuses (e.g., 'pending', 'delivered', 'cancelled'). Return 400 for invalid values.",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization and API correctness — prevents parameter abuse and reduces attack surface."
    },
    {
      "title": "No authorization check — any caller can query any customer's deliveries",
      "risk": "The function trusts the caller-supplied customer_id with no verification that the authenticated user owns that customer_id. This is an IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) allowing cross-customer data access.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries function — missing authz gate before query execution",
      "reproduction": "Authenticate as customer A, then call the endpoint with customer_id=<customer B's ID>. All of customer B's deliveries are returned.",
      "fix": "Extract the authenticated user's customer_id from the session/token and use that instead of (or to validate against) the query parameter. Never trust caller-supplied identity for data scoping.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization — violates principle of least privilege; exposes all customer data to any authenticated user."
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "SELECT * returns all columns including potentially sensitive internal fields",
      "description": "Using SELECT * may leak internal columns (e.g., internal_notes, cost_price, driver_ssn) to the public API response. Explicitly enumerate only the columns the API consumer needs.",
      "location": "search_deliveries query"
    },
    {
      "title": "No pagination or result limit",
      "description": "A query matching thousands of rows will return them all in memory at once. For a public API, this enables denial-of-service by requesting a broad status like 'delivered'. Add LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based pagination.",
      "location": "search_deliveries query and return"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Return a typed data class or dict instead of raw DB row tuples to decouple the API response from the schema",
    "Add logging/metrics on query execution time to detect abuse patterns",
    "Consider adding an index on (customer_id, status) if one doesn't exist — this will be a hot query path"
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "This code is actively exploitable the moment it faces public traffic. SQL injection is the #1 web vulnerability (OWASP A03:2021 Injection).",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe but insufficient — if this code has already been deployed, the database may already be compromised. An incident review and audit are needed.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Switching to parameterized queries is backward-compatible for all legitimate callers. Only malicious payloads will stop working.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No error handling or logging exists. A failed injection attempt or malformed query will surface as an unhandled DB exception with no audit trail.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["auth_security: SQL injection enables full DB compromise; IDOR enables cross-customer data access"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the endpoint or take it offline immediately. If previously deployed, audit DB logs for injection patterns and rotate credentials."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 99,
    "high_confidence_areas": ["SQL injection is unambiguous and trivially exploitable", "IDOR is confirmed by the stated call pattern (customer_id from query params)", "No parameterization or validation exists anywhere in the function"],
    "low_confidence_areas": ["Cannot verify if authorization exists at a middleware layer above this function — but the function itself has none"],
    "assumptions": ["No ORM or query-sanitization layer wraps db_conn.execute", "customer_id and status_filter reach this function unsanitized as stated in the prompt"]
  }
}
